summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}}. A single shared instance is reached through a static accessor, and copy/move operations are suppressed so the uniqueness invariant cannot be broken by accident. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Singleton because {{className}} carries the three structural fingerprints together: a class scope (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}), a static accessor method that returns the shared instance, and explicit deletion of the copy/move operations. Without the deletions, a Singleton's uniqueness invariant is one accidental copy away from breaking, so this evidence is load-bearing.

singleton_class.note: Singleton class boundary — every reference to this class in the rest of the codebase goes through the accessor below. Direct construction is intentionally not how callers reach this object.

static_instance_accessor.note: Static instance — ownership of the unique instance lives on the class itself, not on a caller. The lifetime is tied to the program, which is the trade-off Singleton accepts in exchange for global access.

instance_accessor_method.note: Accessor method — the only legal entry point to the instance. Hiding the constructor and exposing this method is what makes the uniqueness invariant enforceable.

explicit_copy_deletion.note: Copy/move suppression — `= delete` here is not stylistic; it's the line that prevents `auto x = Singleton::instance();` from silently producing a second copy. The Singleton invariant is mechanical, not by convention.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Factory: Factory hands out fresh products per call. {{className}} hands out the same instance every time and explicitly deletes copy/move (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}); the invariant is uniqueness, not selection.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Method Chaining: Method Chaining is about ergonomic mutation of the same instance through chained calls. Singleton is about *reaching* a single instance at all; chaining is unrelated.
